speaker: interviewer
text: Continue about the-- your father selling.

speaker: interviewee
text: How the-- yes, he sold razor blades to the different candy stores. And he, he came home at night and said, you know, it's not bad. The stores all buy them. And what happened was-- well, let me continue. He sold. He went to different candy stores. And he did very well. He's supposed to follow up on those stores a week or two later to see-- to replenish the stock so on and so forth. And the first few days went very well. He sold quite a few of these things. And he was very happy. Then he went back to the stores to follow up on it. And all of a sudden, the store keepers shook their heads. They pointed to the razor blade cardboard. They were still all there, said, I didn't sell any. I can't keep buying them. And he found out that they were mostly Jewish storekeepers. And he-- they bought one carton out of pity, more or less. And of course, you know, times being what they are-- by the way, in those candy stores, they didn't just sell candy. They sold everything. It was actually what a drugstore is today, what the big drug stores are like, only on a much smaller scale. Stationery stores, newspapers, everything was sold in those candy stores. But he found out that after the first one, there was no market for it anymore. Then he sold Fuller brushes, which Fuller brushes were sold from door-to-door. They were very good brushes and very well-known brushes. And they're mostly known for the free samples that they gave out to people. And in fact, for years and years, he had free samples from Fuller Brush Men. But my father went to school there. And he was told, well, you don't give free brushes out when you ring the doorbell. You tell the housewife, he says, I'm a Fuller Brush Man. I have a free sample for you. And that was the in to get into the house. But he was taught the free samples would have to be on the bottom of his case. In order to get to the free samples, he would have to show all and demonstrate all the different brushes and mops and things that they had. And he did that for a while. It was not a good way. You couldn't make a living from it. But he made a few cents here and there that way. And my mother, she became a, a cleaning lady and with very little experience. She was much too slow. Then she-- in the summer time, she went to the beach and sold ice cream at the beach. And I was-- I found out later I was sent to a camp for underprivileged children because they wanted to have me out of the way while they were looking for a job and-- or actually working for, on some sort of a job, if you could call it that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So through some sort of a Jewish federation, they send me to a summer camp for eight weeks, which probably was the best thing that could happen for me, to me. I learned to play baseball. I learned the language. I learned the language as I wasn't supposed to learn. And it was-- I, I-- my English after those eight weeks were so much better. And later on, when I went back to school again, I did very well in school.

speaker: interviewee
text: From then on, my I think-- the first days in school, of course, were torture for me. I didn't know what was going on. I went-- my first day of school was actually five days after arrival here. And I had no idea what went on in the school. And another girl, the daughter of the lady that met us at the boat, she says, oh, you'll, you'll learn it by osmosis. You just listen and keep your eyes open, your ears open. After a while, you'll catch on. And at that age, it was relatively easy for me. I notice that other people who came who were one or two years older than I was had a much more difficult time learning the language. And other people, other youngsters who were younger too-- a year or two younger than I was-- found it much easier to, to learn the language and so on. So I was still in an age where it was relatively easy for me. My parents went to night school to learn English. My father never caught on. He was very unhappy in this country. He never learn English too well. And he never, being an academician, he was not-- he was sort of rigid and was not able to acclimatize himself as well as my-- my mother did marvelously. She did all-- she worked in factories. And she became a sales lady one place. And then she became very successful as a sales lady to very well-known people at Saks in Fifth Avenue. And she had a very strong following and prominent following at Saks Fifth Avenue. And she worked there until she retired for many, many years. And she did very well there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you graduate from high school then in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, first junior high school, and then I went to DeWitt Clinton High school in the Bronx for a while and then we moved to Queens. And I graduated from Newtown High School in Queens.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do after graduation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually I went to City College at night. I had no plans for anything. I knew that I was going to be drafted into the army. That was just the beginning of when United States went into the-- went to war. And I took non-matriculated courses in subjects that interested me-- international law, economics, and accounting, for practical purposes, not to get any degree. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my first job actually after graduating high school was splitting hair in a brush factory. [LAUGHS] Sounds funny today. Brushes were made from horse tails. And we got shipments in of chopped horse tails, more or less. We had to take the hair and process it, bake it. And, and there's a hair splitting machine to split the ends of the hair to make it softer-- the ends softer for the brushes. I lasted about two weeks at that job. [LAUGHS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I understand that Henry Kissinger-- a lot of my so-called career was in parallel to Henry Kissinger's. I also went to dancing school with him at one time. [LAUGHTER] There was a refugee, a, a former gym teacher who became a dancing teacher. And we, as teenagers, were supposed to learn manners and dancing and also meet the proper girls that way. And it was a social sort of thing. And we went to dancing school for awhile. In fact, I have an, a biography of Kissinger. And that school is mentioned in the book, Kissinger. And he never became a star pupil of hers. And later on, I talked to her. And she said he was the most horrible dancer. [? Imagine, ?] he has two left feet. [LAUGHS] And in fact, actually one time, we went on a double date together. He went out with a sister of the girl that I went out with. [LAUGHS] But later on, he also went to high school. He went to the other high school, not to DeWitt Clinton, but to the rival high school. And he went to the army at the same time. And later on, we had sort of parallel careers up to a point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your time in the service.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before I do that, I became a diamond cutter. I worked in the jewelry-- I'll, I'll just shortly go into it. I became a gopher in a jewelry firm as a-- I made $12 a week at that time, which was par for the course for a youngster starting. And I worked there for a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my boss said, you know, they're looking for diamond cutters, diamond cutter apprentices. How would you like to become a diamond cutter apprentice? You can make good money there. So I said, why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: I became a diamond cutter apprentice, worked for $3 a week for three months. Then some government agency decided that that was the equivalent of slavery. And it was illegal. And after three months, we were put on piecework. And we were able to earn the prevailing rate. And we really made a lot of money in those days. The diamond industry, well, it used to be in Antwerp. And it settled here in the States. And people during the war, whenever there was fear of inflation, and invested in diamonds. And it became a very wanted commodity. And they looked for diamond cutters wherever they could find them. And it was a very prosperous occupation at the time. It didn't last very long, because I went into the army. And I was drafted, went to-- had my basic training at Fort McClellan in Alabama during the hot summer. And that was torture. But one day during the-- during some sort of an exercise, they called my name out. I was full of sweat and dirty.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were told to report to some officer somewhere else. And I said, can I take a shower before? I'm filthy, can't go like this. Said, there isn't time. I think you better go the way you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came down there. And there were some others from all over the camp that were put together. And we were put in some sort of a classroom and told we were supposed to take a battery of tests. Apparently when I was drafted into the army, I did rather well. And some tests that we took, I don't even know what they were-- intelligent tests and whatever they were, aptitude tests. I don't know what they were. And we were given a battery of tests and had no idea what they were for. And after the tests were over, we went back to what we did before. And I forgot all about it. And shortly before the end of our basic training, I was again called somewhere on the post to report. And this time, I was supposed to dress up in dress uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went there. And they interviewed me. And they said, well, you did very well in those tests. And I had, I had forgotten. I think I asked, what tests? And said, those tests that you took were about this and this and that. And we'd like to send you to University of Alabama where you're going to get another set of tests. So I was very happy, because I heard that we were supposed to be shipped out to some sort of an infantry division. And anything to get out of the infantry was, was something to look forward to. So I went there. And we were actually treated like gentlemen rather than like ordinary soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to the-- to Tuscaloosa to the University of Alabama, were given a battery of tests. I remember one test in particular I had a lot of fun with was a language aptitude test. We were given an imaginary language, an imaginary set of vocabularies, an imaginary set of grammatical rules, and then were given a paragraph to translate into that imaginary language and another paragraph from that imaginary language to translate into English. And I thought that was, was great. Of course, I was always very, very conscious of language and grammar, having studied Latin a little bit and French and also English. So I had no problem with that. And apparently, I did fairly well. And they sent me to army specialized training program at Haverford College.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, they had a section teaching German and Italian. So I had my choice. Said, do you want to go to the German section or do you want to go to the Italian section? So I said, well, can I-- shall I go to the German section and am able to goof off, or I'll go to the Italian section. Might as well learn the language, learn-- get something out of it. So I opted for the Italian section.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we took a very concentrated course in everything from language-- in fact, after a few weeks, we were not allowed to talk English anymore. We got demerits whenever we were caught speaking English. We had to-- everything had to be in Italian. There were Italian professors who taught us Italian history and culture in Italian towards the end. We didn't get everything that they said. But they talked slowly. And it was a very, very concentrated and interesting course. And on weekends, I was able to go home, which was nice, too. It was a few hours by train. And at the end of the course, just before the course, a week or so before, the commanding officer called a few of us in. And he said, I need six volunteers. In the army, you don't volunteer. But that commanding officer, he was actually a civilian in uniform more than an officer. And we were very-- we had a lot of respect for him and trusted his judgment. He says, I can't tell you what it is. But if, if it would be me, I would volunteer. So that was good enough for me. I volunteered. And we were sent to a debarking camp in Virginia and sent overseas to-- on a ship, in an old, creaking ship, in what I later found out to be the biggest convoy they ever had across the Atlantic. That was right during the U-boat season. And we made it safely across the Atlantic. And we were sent to the-- to Heaton Moor, which is a suburb of Manchester, England, and billeted in a house. And we took courses at the University of Manchester in civil affairs and military government. We were told that when we landed in France, our-- we would be divided into detachments. And we would, while in France, would run towns and cities and so on as civil affairs. And as soon as we crossed the border, we would become a military government. And we were-- at the university, we had to do research on a landkreis in Germany. And it was in such a detail that we knew exactly where everything was, where the police station was, where the government, who the governor was, and what his duties were, what the duty of this person was, and so on. The only thing is the army, being what it was, I think I was the only soldier in the United States who actually landed in that landkreis that we studied. Everyone-- we also, not only that one, but we also had three landkreise, which were counties, and that we had to run later on. In France, we had to-- we followed the troops just as they were advancing. We were always right behind them, setting up the water, the utilities, some semblance of government. And then we moved to the next town. And later on, as the armies advanced, there were displaced persons. We had to run displaced person camps. And we ran one of the biggest one, of, of all places in Trier, the town where my mother comes from. In fact, there was a big article written about that camp in I may still have it someplace-- and also in magazine. And it was one of the largest camps. There were lots of experiences in that, in that camp. But I don't know. It's going get a little bit too involved here, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us just one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us just one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember when President Roosevelt died. That was when we were in one of those camps. And it was very touching. People constantly came up to us and gave us their condolences. I still choke up when I hear about it. [SOBBING] It, it was very, very touching. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was as if, as if he-- as if they lost one of their champions. That's one of those things that I think had the greatest impact. Of course, there were other. There were terrific-- the attitude-- they were Indians. They were French. They were former prisoners of war. And it was very difficult. It was one Babel, actually. Every country was represented. There were former French prisoners of war who went through the war and suffered terribly and completely demoralized. They couldn't care less about anything. And we felt very sorry for them. We couldn't get them to clean up. We couldn't get them to do anything. They were very uncommunicative. They were almost completely disoriented almost. And yet, there were Yugoslavian soldiers who had the most fantastic discipline and helped whenever they could, and others. And there were Russians there. At night, there was music. There was dancing. At one time, of course, there was some gunfire in the distance. And a rumor was started that the Germans were counter-attacking. And oh, there was panic. And well, we told them we didn't know. We told them it was our guns. And they were being [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] sent back. Before that, by the way, I was also in the Battle of the Bulge, at which time, I was made an MP. They didn't need civil affairs at that time. They needed policemen because they needed special people to screen Germans. Because there were German soldiers that were in American uniforms and trying to confuse our groups. They tried to turn directional signs around so that we would be completely confused. And for a while they succeeded, but not for very long.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was just a, a, a thought. But in military and civil affairs, when we went into Germany, we went into Bavaria. And the-- we covered the kreis of Hergstadt, Neustadt, and Scheinfeld. And that was just at the time of the German collapse.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, we-- our offices were mostly interested in setting up the local government, make sure that the electricity was working, that the water was running, that there was gas. And I was not that happy about it. And I was more concerned with getting rid of the Nazis that were there and getting, you know, getting rid of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- and I talked to the officers about it. And I said, you know, aren't you going to do anything about that? That's just as important as getting the electricity. They said, yeah, I guess you're right. But none of us knows anything about the, the Nazis. You speak the language. Why don't you take care of that? So I was on the T5 at the time, corporal. And they gave me an office. They gave me a secretary. And they gave me all the support that I needed. And I just ran the whole office. They gave me an assistant from the OSS, I finally got. And we got-- we went through all of the government offices [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And I found a very effective way of screening them. I got hold of the local newspaper, the archives. They had a newspaper beautifully bound in individual volumes from the '20s to the, to the last day it was published. And in each section were the party activities-- who spoke, who did what, and so on. And I went through this whole list of the women's organization, the NSDAP notices. And I made a card index of every single person that was mentioned. And in fact, I have one of those card indexes here. I-- and that later on became quite a list. And all the officials had to fill out questionnaires-- when they joined the party, what they did, whether they ever held any speeches, and so on. And we were able to check that against the, the records. And it was-- the Germans were so amazed. I said, you said you joined the party in 1938. Yet in 1933, you held a speech at this and this place. And you mentioned this and that. And some of them were dumbfounded. They had forgotten all about that.
